question: How has CSAT trended over the selected time period?
logic: Aggregate CSAT by equal time periods such as week or month. Use rolling averages to smooth noise, then fit a regression trend line to determine whether the direction is upward, downward, or flat. Validate whether current-period movement is statistically meaningful before calling it improvement or decline.
statistics: Mean by period, Median by period, Survey Count, Rolling Average, Standard Deviation, Trend Slope, R², 95% Confidence Interval, Period-over-Period Change, t-test or Mann-Whitney U Test
guardrail: Say “trending up/down” only when the slope and/or period comparison is meaningful. Otherwise say performance is broadly stable.

question: What is the overall CSAT performance during the selected period?
logic: Summarize overall performance using both central tendency and uncertainty. Do not report only the average; include dispersion and reliability.
statistics: Mean, Median, Standard Deviation, Variance, Minimum, Maximum, Range, Q1, Q3, IQR, Survey Count, Standard Error, 95% Confidence Interval
guardrail: The overall CSAT is reliable only when sample size is adequate and confidence interval is reasonably narrow.

question: Which managers are performing the best, and how statistically reliable are their results?
logic: Rank managers by mean CSAT only after applying a minimum survey threshold. Use confidence intervals to determine whether apparent ranking differences are reliable.
statistics: Manager Mean, Median, Survey Count, Standard Deviation, CV, Percentile Rank, 95% Confidence Interval, ANOVA or Kruskal-Wallis
guardrail: Avoid ranking managers with low survey volume as definitive winners.

question: Which agents are performing the best, and how statistically reliable are their results?
logic: Rank agents by mean CSAT after applying sample-size thresholds. Compare confidence intervals to avoid misleading rankings from small samples.
statistics: Agent Mean, Median, Survey Count, Standard Deviation, CV, Percentile Rank, 95% Confidence Interval
guardrail: An agent with 5 perfect surveys should not automatically outrank an agent with 200 strong surveys.

question: Which managers have shown the greatest statistically significant improvement over time?
logic: Compare equal historical periods and measure both size and significance of change. Confirm that improvement is not caused by random variation.
statistics: Previous Period Mean, Current Period Mean, Absolute Change, % Change, Trend Slope, Effect Size, p-value, 95% Confidence Interval
guardrail: Call out improvement only when direction, size, and reliability align.

question: Which managers have experienced the most significant decline over time?
logic: Compare equal periods and detect sustained decline using trend and significance testing. Avoid treating one low period as a decline unless supported statistically.
statistics: Previous Mean, Current Mean, Absolute Decline, % Decline, Trend Slope, Effect Size, p-value, Survey Count
guardrail: Flag as decline when there is enough volume and a meaningful negative movement.

question: Which agents have demonstrated the greatest improvement over the selected period?
logic: Calculate each agent’s recent vs historical CSAT and validate with trend and effect size. Use minimum sample threshold for both periods.
statistics: Previous Mean, Current Mean, Absolute Change, % Improvement, Trend Slope, Effect Size, p-value, Survey Count
guardrail: Improvement must be sustained, not just one high-scoring survey.

question: Which agents have experienced the greatest decline over the selected period?
logic: Identify agents with negative movement across comparable periods, supported by sufficient survey count and statistical evidence.
statistics: Previous Mean, Current Mean, % Decline, Trend Slope, Effect Size, p-value, 95% Confidence Interval
guardrail: Use “needs review” rather than “poor performance” if sample is limited.

question: Which managers have maintained the most consistent CSAT performance over time?
logic: Measure stability separately from performance. A manager is consistent when CSAT variation is low across periods.
statistics: Standard Deviation, Variance, CV, IQR, Rolling Standard Deviation, Control Limits
guardrail: A high average is not the same as consistency; use variability metrics.

question: Which managers exhibit the highest variability in CSAT performance?
logic: Identify managers whose scores fluctuate more than peers or historical norms. Use variability and outlier measures.
statistics: Standard Deviation, CV, Range, IQR, Outlier Count, Rolling SD
guardrail: High volatility means inconsistent outcomes, not necessarily low performance.

question: Which agents have maintained the most consistent CSAT performance?
logic: Measure each agent’s score dispersion over time. Filter for adequate survey volume before judging consistency.
statistics: Agent Standard Deviation, Variance, CV, IQR, Rolling SD, Survey Count
guardrail: Consistency should not be calculated seriously for very low survey counts.

question: Which agents exhibit the highest variability in CSAT performance?
logic: Rank agents by dispersion metrics and identify those with frequent high-low swings.
statistics: Standard Deviation, CV, Range, IQR, Outlier Count
guardrail: Volatility is a coaching signal only when sample size is adequate.

question: Which weeks or months recorded the highest and lowest CSAT, and were these differences statistically meaningful?
logic: Aggregate CSAT by time period, rank periods, and compare top/bottom periods using confidence intervals or significance tests.
statistics: Period Mean, Median, Survey Count, 95% Confidence Interval, Ranking, t-test or Mann-Whitney U
guardrail: Do not overinterpret best/worst period if confidence intervals overlap heavily.

question: Were there any statistically significant spikes or drops in CSAT during the selected period?
logic: Compare each period against rolling/historical average and detect abnormal variation using outlier and process control methods.
statistics: Z-score, IQR Outlier Detection, Rolling Mean, Rolling SD, Upper/Lower Control Limits
guardrail: Label as spike/drop only when outside expected variation.

question: Which managers consistently outperform the organizational average?
logic: Compare each manager’s mean against the organizational mean across periods and validate whether the difference is reliable.
statistics: Manager Mean, Organization Mean, Difference, Percentile Rank, 95% Confidence Interval, ANOVA/Post-hoc Tests
guardrail: Outperformance should be consistent across time, not just one reporting period.

question: Which agents consistently outperform the organizational average?
logic: Compare each agent’s mean CSAT to the organizational average while accounting for survey count and uncertainty.
statistics: Agent Mean, Organization Mean, Difference, Percentile Rank, 95% Confidence Interval
guardrail: Use confidence-adjusted interpretation for low-volume agents.

question: How is CSAT distributed across managers, and what does it reveal about overall performance?
logic: Analyze the shape and spread of manager-level CSAT rather than relying only on averages.
statistics: Manager Mean Distribution, Median, Quartiles, IQR, Standard Deviation, Skewness, Kurtosis
guardrail: Distribution helps show whether performance is concentrated or uneven.

question: How is CSAT distributed across agents, and are there any noticeable patterns or outliers?
logic: Analyze agent-level CSAT distribution to identify concentration, spread, skew, and outliers.
statistics: Agent Mean Distribution, Median, Quartiles, IQR, Standard Deviation, Skewness, Kurtosis, Outlier Count
guardrail: Outliers need review but should not be treated as root cause without more data.

question: Which managers have the most balanced team performance?
logic: Within each manager, compare the variation among agent-level averages. Low spread indicates balanced performance.
statistics: Team Mean, Agent Mean SD within Team, Team CV, Team Range, Team IQR
guardrail: Balanced team means agents under the manager perform similarly.

question: Which managers have the greatest variation in team performance?
logic: Within each manager, measure the performance gap between agents and overall spread.
statistics: Highest Agent Mean, Lowest Agent Mean, Range, SD, IQR, CV
guardrail: Large gaps suggest uneven performance distribution, not necessarily manager causality.

question: Who are the Top 10 performing agents based on statistically reliable performance?
logic: Apply minimum sample threshold, rank agents by mean CSAT, and include confidence intervals to prevent misleading ranks.
statistics: Mean, Survey Count, 95% Confidence Interval, Percentile Rank, Reliability Flag
guardrail: Prefer confidence-adjusted ranking when sample sizes differ widely.

question: Who are the Bottom 10 performing agents based on statistically reliable performance?
logic: Identify agents with the lowest statistically reliable CSAT, considering sample size and uncertainty.
statistics: Mean, Survey Count, 95% Confidence Interval, Percentile Rank, Reliability Flag
guardrail: Low ranking should be shown as “requires review” when sample is small.

question: Which agents have consistently improved month over month?
logic: Calculate monthly means by agent and check whether slope is positive across multiple months, not just between two points.
statistics: Monthly Mean, Trend Slope, R², Month-over-Month Change, Positive Period Count, Survey Count
guardrail: Sustained trend requires multiple periods with adequate volume.

question: Which agents should be prioritized for coaching based on statistically significant performance trends?
logic: Prioritize agents with low mean CSAT, negative trend, high variability, or statistically significant decline. Do not infer the cause of low CSAT.
statistics: Mean, Trend Slope, SD, Percentile Rank, Confidence Interval, Survey Count, Effect Size, p-value
guardrail: Say “coaching priority based on CSAT pattern,” not “agent caused dissatisfaction.”

question: Which managers should be prioritized for performance improvement based on team performance trends?
logic: Flag managers whose teams show low mean CSAT, negative trend, high variability, or many low-performing agents.
statistics: Team Mean, Trend Slope, Team SD, Team Confidence Interval, Survey Count, % Agents Below Target
guardrail: This identifies teams needing attention, not proven managerial cause.

question: How many agents are currently performing above the CSAT target?
logic: Compare each agent’s mean CSAT against the target after applying sample threshold. Summarize count and percentage.
statistics: Agent Mean, Target, Count Above Target, % Above Target, Survey Count, Confidence Interval
guardrail: Use target comparison only if a valid CSAT target is defined.

question: How many agents are currently performing below the CSAT target?
logic: Compare each agent’s mean CSAT against target and quantify below-target population.
statistics: Agent Mean, Target, Count Below Target, % Below Target, Survey Count, Confidence Interval
guardrail: Separate confirmed below-target from low-confidence below-target cases.

question: Which manager has the highest proportion of high-performing agents?
logic: For each manager, calculate the share of agents above target and compare proportions with team size context.
statistics: % Agents Above Target, Count Above Target, Team Size, Confidence Interval for Proportion, Percentile Rank
guardrail: A small team can show high percentage but lower reliability.

question: What statistically significant changes have occurred compared to the previous reporting period?
logic: Compare current and previous periods across overall, manager, and agent levels using equal windows and significance testing.
statistics: Mean Difference, % Change, Effect Size, p-value, Confidence Interval, Trend Comparison, Survey Count
guardrail: Report only changes that are both statistically and practically meaningful.

question: What are the key statistically significant insights and recommendations from the current reporting period?
logic: Summarize only insights supported by data: trend, significant changes, high/low performers, stability, volatility, outliers, and reliability.
statistics: Overall Mean, Trend Slope, R², SD, Confidence Intervals, Effect Size, Outlier Count, Survey Count, Reliability Flags
guardrail: Recommendations must be action-oriented but not causal unless supported by other data.

question: Which managers have the highest confidence in their CSAT performance based on sample size?
logic: Assess reliability of each manager’s CSAT estimate using sample size and confidence interval width.
statistics: Survey Count, Standard Error, 95% Confidence Interval Width, SD, Reliability Score
guardrail: High confidence means narrow uncertainty, not necessarily high CSAT.

question: Which agents have results that may be unreliable due to low survey volume?
logic: Flag agents whose sample size is below threshold or whose confidence intervals are too wide for reliable interpretation.
statistics: Survey Count, Minimum Threshold, Standard Error, CI Width, Reliability Flag
guardrail: These agents should be excluded or marked low-confidence in rankings.

question: Which managers are consistently improving despite fluctuations in individual survey scores?
logic: Use rolling averages and regression slope to identify upward direction even when individual scores vary.
statistics: Rolling Mean, Trend Slope, R², Rolling SD, Period Means, p-value
guardrail: Improving despite fluctuation means positive trend with manageable variability.

question: Which agents have demonstrated sustained improvement over multiple reporting periods?
logic: Identify agents with positive movement across several consecutive periods, supported by trend slope and adequate data.
statistics: Monthly/Weekly Mean, Consecutive Positive Change Count, Trend Slope, R², Survey Count
guardrail: Sustained improvement requires repeated positive periods, not one jump.

question: Which managers have statistically stable performance despite changes in survey volume?
logic: Evaluate whether manager CSAT remains within expected control limits even when survey counts vary.
statistics: Period Mean, Survey Count by Period, Rolling SD, Control Limits, CV, CI Width
guardrail: Stable means variation remains within expected statistical boundaries.

question: Which agents have recently become performance outliers?
logic: Compare recent agent CSAT against historical agent average and organizational distribution to detect new outliers.
statistics: Recent Mean, Historical Mean, Z-score, Modified Z-score, IQR Rule, Outlier Flag
guardrail: Recent outlier status should be validated with sample count.

question: Which teams show the greatest performance consistency across all agents?
logic: Measure variation of agent-level averages within each manager’s team and rank teams by lowest dispersion.
statistics: Agent Mean SD within Team, Team CV, Team IQR, Range, Survey Count
guardrail: Consistent team performance means low dispersion among agents.

question: Which teams display unusually high variation that warrants further investigation?
logic: Identify teams with within-team variation significantly higher than the organizational team variation benchmark.
statistics: Team SD, Team CV, Team Range, Team IQR, Z-score of Team Variability
guardrail: High variation suggests uneven team performance, not cause.

question: Which managers have experienced the largest statistically significant performance shifts?
logic: Compare manager current vs historical means and rank by absolute statistically significant movement.
statistics: Current Mean, Historical Mean, Absolute Difference, Effect Size, p-value, CI, Trend Slope
guardrail: Separate positive shifts and negative shifts for leadership clarity.

question: Which agents are closest to crossing the target performance threshold?
logic: Calculate the gap between each agent’s mean CSAT and target, then identify those within a narrow band above or below target.
statistics: Agent Mean, Target Gap, Confidence Interval, Survey Count, Percentile Rank
guardrail: Useful for near-target coaching or recognition; avoid overprecision if CI is wide.

question: Which managers have maintained above-average CSAT throughout the reporting period?
logic: Check whether each manager’s period-level CSAT remains above organizational average across multiple periods.
statistics: Period Mean, Organization Mean by Period, % Periods Above Average, Survey Count, CI
guardrail: Consistent above-average requires repeated performance across periods.

question: Which agents have maintained above-average CSAT throughout the reporting period?
logic: Check agent period-level CSAT against organizational average over time with sample threshold.
statistics: Agent Period Mean, Org Period Mean, % Periods Above Average, Survey Count, CI
guardrail: Exclude periods with too few surveys from consistency claims.

question: Are the differences between managers statistically significant or likely due to random variation?
logic: Compare multiple manager groups using an appropriate omnibus test, then use post-hoc comparisons where needed.
statistics: ANOVA if assumptions hold, Kruskal-Wallis if non-normal, p-value, Effect Size, Post-hoc Tests, CI
guardrail: If not significant, say differences may be random/noise.

question: Are the differences between agents statistically significant or likely due to random variation?
logic: Compare agent groups carefully, only for agents meeting sample thresholds, and avoid overtesting without correction.
statistics: ANOVA/Kruskal-Wallis, p-value, Effect Size, Post-hoc Tests, Multiple Comparison Correction, CI
guardrail: Use caution because many agents can create false positives.

question: Which time periods demonstrated the highest overall performance stability?
logic: Measure period-level variation and identify periods with low dispersion and reliable sample size.
statistics: Period Mean, Period SD, CV, IQR, Survey Count, Control Limits
guardrail: Stable periods have lower variation, not necessarily the highest mean.

question: Which time periods exhibited the greatest performance volatility?
logic: Identify periods with high dispersion, large swings, or many outlier scores.
statistics: Period SD, CV, Range, IQR, Outlier Count, Rolling SD
guardrail: Volatile periods need investigation but cause cannot be inferred from available fields.

question: Are recent improvements part of a sustained trend or short-term fluctuations?
logic: Compare recent movement against longer historical trend and rolling averages. Use trend slope and R² to separate sustained signal from noise.
statistics: Recent Mean, Historical Mean, Rolling Average, Trend Slope, R², p-value, Control Limits
guardrail: Call it sustained only when multiple periods support the same direction.

question: Which agents have the highest confidence-adjusted performance rankings?
logic: Rank agents using mean CSAT adjusted by uncertainty, such as lower confidence bound, to reward reliable high performance.
statistics: Mean, Survey Count, Standard Error, 95% CI, Lower Confidence Bound, Percentile Rank
guardrail: Confidence-adjusted ranking reduces small-sample bias.

question: Which managers have the strongest combination of high performance and high consistency?
logic: Create a composite view using high mean CSAT and low variability, while enforcing reliability thresholds.
statistics: Manager Mean, SD, CV, Survey Count, Confidence Interval, Percentile Rank, Composite Score
guardrail: Best managers should be both high-performing and stable.

question: Based on the statistical analysis, what are the top priorities for leadership attention?
logic: Prioritize areas with statistically reliable low performance, significant decline, high volatility, or large gaps. Weight insights by reliability and practical impact.
statistics: Mean Gap vs Target, Trend Slope, Effect Size, p-value, SD/CV, Survey Count, Reliability Score
guardrail: Priorities should be evidence-based and phrased as focus areas, not causes.
